---
title: "Right-Sizing 101: Getting Your Pods to Ask for What They Actually Need"
---

<!-- Generated by transform-chapter.ts with openai-compatible/MiniMax-M2 -->
<!-- Density: standard | Word target: 2500-3500 -->

<!-- Density: standard | Word target: 2500-3500 -->

Your Kubernetes clusters are quietly burning through budget you never meant to spend. Research from Sedai reveals a stark reality: 96% of enterprises have adopted Kubernetes, yet only 13% of requested CPU actually powers the workloads running on those clusters. The gap between what teams ask for and what their applications consume creates an estimated 30% waste across the industry (Sedai).

This is not a team problem. It is a structural one. Most organizations configure resource requests based on guesswork, initial testing, or legacy assumptions rather than actual runtime behavior. The mathematics are straightforward—workloads typically consume between 20-45% of their requested resources—and the financial implications compound rapidly. One mid-sized e-commerce operation discovered it was spending $89,000 per month on Kubernetes infrastructure before right-sizing brought that figure down to $52,000 within six weeks. A fintech company achieved comparable results, moving from $34,000 to $21,000 in just four weeks. These outcomes are not exceptions; they represent the predictable return when configuration aligns with reality.

This chapter builds your capability to close that gap systematically. You will learn to measure what your workloads actually consume using p95 metrics rather than averages, implement Vertical Pod Autoscaler recommendations that adapt requests automatically as traffic patterns shift, enforce quota guardrails that prevent regression, and layer scheduled scaling for workloads with predictable demand cycles. The tooling exists to automate much of this—platforms like zopnight surface utilization patterns across namespaces, translate Vertical Pod Autoscaler output into actionable recommendations without manual kubectl exploration, and visualize quota enforcement status so teams can move from data to decision faster.

Right-sizing is the highest-leverage first step in any Kubernetes cost optimization journey. Before you explore spot instances, cluster autoscaling, or workload migration strategies, ensure you are not paying for resources your applications do not use. The foundation matters, and it is far easier to build than you might expect.

## Why Your Pods Are Over-Provisioned by Default

When a pod lands on a node, Kubernetes makes two critical decisions based on values you may never have consciously set: where to place it and what happens when it runs amok. These decisions hinge on resource requests and limits, two configuration fields that sound similar but serve fundamentally different purposes.

A request tells the scheduler the minimum amount of CPU or memory a container needs to function. Think of it as your pod's baseline guarantee—the cluster uses this value to find a node with enough free capacity. If your pod requests 500 millicores and the node has 400 millicores available, the scheduler looks elsewhere. A limit, by contrast, sets a hard ceiling. When a container hits its memory limit, it gets terminated. When it hits its CPU limit, it gets throttled. The limit is your escape rope, not your operating target.

| | Request | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Scheduler guarantee for placement | Hard ceiling for runtime behavior |
| **Effect** | Determines which node pods land on | Determines what happens at peak consumption |
| **Default (if unset)** | 0 (unbounded) | Unlimited (unbounded) |

This default behavior is where the trouble begins. When you install a Helm chart or deploy a sample manifest, Kubernetes does not assign sensible defaults—it assigns none. A request of zero means the scheduler treats your pod as effectively free to place anywhere. A limit of unlimited means your container can consume every available resource on its node. Developers, understandably nervous about OOM kills or CPU throttling, tend to mirror production-grade specs they see in tutorials or past projects. A simple API service might request 256Mi of memory in a quick-start guide; a cautious engineer bumps that to 2Gi "just to be safe."

The cumulative effect is staggering. Research from Sedai establishes that organizations average just 13% CPU utilization across their clusters—the other 87% represents requested but unused capacity. This is not theoretical waste. A separate study from Arc82 found that 88% of teams report year-over-year increases in their Kubernetes total cost of ownership, with resource over-provisioning cited as a primary driver. The math is brutal: if you request 10 CPUs but use 1.3, you are paying for 8.7 CPUs doing nothing.

The defaults baked into popular charts, the safety margins baked into human intuition, and the absence of visibility into actual consumption patterns combine into a self-reinforcing cycle. You request too much, the cluster accommodates, and the next team member copies your manifest as a template. Breaking this cycle requires measuring what workloads actually consume rather than what teams assume they need—and that measurement is where most organizations stall.

## Seeing the Real Picture: p95 vs. Average Usage

The numbers from the previous section paint an uncomfortable picture: 13% average CPU utilization across clusters, 87% of requested capacity sitting idle. But that average conceals a deeper problem. Most workloads do not consume resources steadily—they surge, then dormant, then surge again. An e-commerce checkout service handling Black Friday traffic looks nothing like the same service at 3 AM. A batch processing job might use zero CPU for minutes, then spike to full capacity for seconds, then finish. When you right-size based on averages, you are sizing for a fictional steady-state that likely never existed.

Average utilization tells you what a container consumes over time, but it says nothing about what it needs at any given moment. A pod averaging 200mCPU could actually spike to 2000mCPU twice daily, enough to trigger throttling or eviction if you sized based on that 200mCPU average. This is the bursty reality of most production workloads, and it is why right-sizing without understanding peak behavior is guesswork dressed in metrics.

This is where percentile-based measurement changes everything. The p95 metric captures the value below which 95% of your observations fall—in practical terms, it represents the ceiling your container needs to handle normal operation without contention. You are planning for the 5% of moments when demand peaks, not the hypothetical average of all moments. For most applications, p95 utilization will be 3-5x higher than average, sometimes dramatically more for event-driven or periodic workloads.

Querying this data is straightforward with the right tooling. If you already run Prometheus, a simple query retrieves p99 CPU across any window:

This query expression returns the 99th percentile CPU usage in millicores over the selected time range, giving you a clear view of peak demand rather than smoothed averages. For teams without Prometheus, kubectl top pods provides real-time current usage, and cloud dashboards like AWS CloudWatch or GCP Monitoring offer similar percentile views with built-in visualization.

The measurement phase is not optional—it is the prerequisite that makes every subsequent optimization step viable. Before you apply Vertical Pod Autoscaler recommendations or set quota guardrails, you need to know what your workloads actually demand. Platforms like zopnight automate this discovery phase by surfacing p95 versus average utilization across all namespaces, translating raw data into VPA-driven recommendations, and visualizing quota status so teams can act on measurements rather than assumptions.

Without p95, you are right-sizing in the dark. With it, you have the foundation for every optimization that follows.

## Vertical Pod Autoscaling: Automation That Respects Stability

Vertical Pod Autoscaler addresses the measurement-to-action gap directly. Where p95 analysis reveals what your pods actually need, VPA translates those insights into concrete resource requests automatically. The mechanism works by analyzing historical utilization, comparing it against current requests, and proposing updated values—or applying them automatically depending on which mode you configure.

VPA offers four distinct modes that control how aggressively it intervenes. **Off** mode generates recommendations without modifying any pods; it simply tells you what requests should be. This is the safest starting point for production environments where stability trumps optimization. **Initial** mode applies recommendations only when a pod is first created, then leaves it alone—useful for establishing sensible baselines on new deployments without ongoing disruption. **Auto** mode monitors running pods and recreates them to apply new values when recommendations change significantly. This is where the 20-40% cost savings materialize, but it comes with a trade-off: pods restart whenever VPA decides to adjust their resources. **Recreate** is an experimental mode that behaves like Auto but applies recommendations more aggressively, destroying and recreating pods even for minor adjustments.

The restart requirement is the critical consideration. Each time VPA triggers a pod recreation, you incur a brief availability blip—acceptable for stateless workloads, potentially problematic for latency-sensitive services. Most teams find that starting with Off or Initial mode provides the data needed to validate recommendations before enabling Auto mode. Once confidence builds, graduating to Auto for non-mission-critical workloads like batch processors, development environments, or analytics jobs delivers meaningful savings without risking production stability.

The VPA configuration lives in a custom resource definition that specifies which pods to target and what boundaries to respect. You define minimum and maximum allowed values to prevent VPA from suggesting impractical changes—constraining memory between 256Mi and 4Gi, for instance, keeps recommendations within reasonable bounds. The updateMode field determines the behavior, and you can scope the recommendation to specific containers within a deployment rather than applying blanket values.

What makes this approach powerful is combining automated measurement with actionable recommendations. The previous section established that p95 analysis reveals actual demand patterns; VPA takes those patterns and produces concrete configuration changes. Without tooling to bridge the gap, teams typically resort to manual kubectl commands, grepping through metrics and iterating on values—a process that does not scale beyond a handful of workloads.

Platforms like zopnight accelerate this workflow by surfacing VPA-driven recommendations automatically, visualizing quota enforcement status, and presenting recommendations alongside the utilization data that validates them. Instead of exploring kubectl output manually, teams receive pre-computed suggestions tied directly to the measurements they already trust. This transforms right-sizing from a periodic audit exercise into an ongoing, data-driven practice that keeps resource requests aligned with actual consumption as workloads evolve.

```{.d2 width="100%" file="diagrams/vpa-workflow.d2"}
```

*How VPA observes actual resource consumption and right-sizes pod requests to eliminate waste*


## Namespace Resource Quotas: Guardrails That Scale

Even the most carefully tuned Vertical Pod Autoscaler settings cannot protect against a single misconfigured CronJob spawning thousands of pods overnight, or a developer accidentally applying a deployment that requests 50 cores. This is where namespace-level guardrails become essential—not as a replacement for right-sizing, but as the safety net that prevents runaway workloads from consuming your entire cluster.

Kubernetes provides two complementary mechanisms for namespace-level controls. **ResourceQuota** establishes hard caps on the total resources a namespace can consume, acting as a hard ceiling that no workload within that namespace can exceed. If your production namespace has a ResourceQuota of 100 cores and 200Gi memory, every pod combined cannot request more than that total, regardless of individual settings. **LimitRange** operates at a different granularity: it enforces minimum and maximum requests per pod or container, and can automatically inject default resource requests into pods that omit them. Where ResourceQuota protects the cluster from aggregate overcommitment, LimitRange protects against individual misconfigurations.

A typical ResourceQuota for a production namespace might cap CPU at 200 cores and memory at 500Gi, with additional limits on persistent volume claims or the total number of pods. The configuration is straightforward: you apply it once per namespace, and Kubernetes automatically rejects any deployment that would push the namespace over its allocated budget. For teams running multi-tenant clusters, this is the difference between a rogue workload causing an incident and that workload being rejected at admission time.

LimitRange becomes valuable when you want to enforce defaults without requiring every development team to specify requests manually. A LimitRange can automatically set default CPU requests of 100m and memory requests of 128Mi for any pod that does not define them, while also preventing anyone from requesting more than 8 cores or 32Gi per container. This ensures that even the fastest-moving teams cannot accidentally deploy unsized workloads.

The 10-20% savings from quota enforcement come not from optimizing efficient workloads, but from preventing the catastrophic spikes that render clusters unusable. An e-commerce platform that right-sizes its production pods to p95 metrics still loses money if a single nightly batch job consumes all available memory and crashes checkout services. Quotas isolate those failures.

For organizations with multiple teams, dev/prod environments, or customer-facing multi-tenant offerings, namespace-level quotas are not optional—they are the operational foundation that makes right-sizing possible at scale. With tools like zopnight, teams can visualize quota consumption across namespaces, identify which teams are approaching their limits, and receive alerts before overcommitment becomes a crisis. This visibility transforms quotas from a policy checkbox into an active guardrail that keeps your optimization investments intact.

```yaml
# ResourceQuota: enforce aggregate resource ceiling for a team namespace
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: team-quota
  namespace: payments-team
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "20"       # Total CPU requests capped at 20 cores
    requests.memory: 40Gi    # Total memory requests capped at 40Gi
    limits.cpu: "40"
    limits.memory: 80Gi
    pods: "50"               # Hard pod count ceiling
---
# LimitRange: per-pod defaults to prevent containers with no requests
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: team-limit-range
  namespace: payments-team
spec:
  limits:
  - type: Container
    default:
      cpu: "500m"
      memory: "512Mi"
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "100m"
      memory: "128Mi"
    max:
      cpu: "4"
      memory: "8Gi"
```

## Cron-Based Cluster Scaling: Time-Based Efficiency

While VPA optimizes how much each individual pod requests, and ResourceQuota prevents aggregate overcommitment, neither addresses the most predictable source of waste: environments that sit completely unused for hours or even days at a time. Development, staging, and test clusters routinely consume budget during business hours only to sit idle overnight, on weekends, and during holidays. This predictable idleness represents the lowest-hanging fruit in any Kubernetes cost optimization strategy.

Rather than manually scaling node pools down at 7pm and back up at 7am, organizations automate this cycle through scheduled cluster scaling. A Karpenter NodePool with a time-based schedule removes idle nodes without touching running pods:

```yaml
# Karpenter NodePool: scale dev node pool to zero on weeknights and weekends
apiVersion: karpenter.sh/v1
kind: NodePool
metadata:
  name: dev-spot
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      nodeClassRef:
        name: default
      taints:
        - key: "env"
          value: "dev"
          effect: NoSchedule
  disruption:
    consolidationPolicy: WhenEmpty
    consolidateAfter: 30s
    budgets:
      # Scale to zero weeknights 7pm–7am and all weekend
      - nodes: "0"
        schedule: "0 19 * * 1-5"   # Mon–Fri at 7pm
        duration: 12h
      - nodes: "0"
        schedule: "0 0 * * 6"      # Saturday midnight → all weekend
        duration: 48h
```

Karpenter, GKE, and AKS all support cron-based node pool schedules that resize capacity based on calendar patterns. A simple expression like `0 19 * * 1-5` shuts down non-essential nodes at 7pm weekdays, while `0 7 * * 1-5` brings them back Monday through Friday morning. Development and staging environments can extend this further—scaling to zero throughout weekends and holidays.

The 65-75% savings on eligible workloads materialize because you are not merely optimizing requests; you are actually deprovisioning idle infrastructure. An e-commerce platform reduced dev/test spending from $89,000 to $52,000 monthly in six weeks through scheduled scaling alone (Source: Customer Data). A fintech company achieved $34,000 to $

## Choosing Your Path: A Right-Sizing Decision Framework

These three approaches to Kubernetes cost optimization are not competing alternatives—they form a complementary stack, each addressing a different dimension of waste. Understanding where each one delivers the greatest impact allows teams to sequence their efforts for maximum return with minimum operational friction.

The Vertical Pod Autoscaler targets the pod level, recommending precise CPU and memory requests based on actual consumption patterns. It delivers 20-40% savings on production stateless applications but requires pod restarts to apply new recommendations, making it best suited for workloads that tolerate brief disruptions. Resource quotas operate at the namespace level, establishing hard ceilings that prevent any single team or workload from consuming the entire cluster. They yield 10-20% savings with low implementation effort and excel in multi-tenant environments where preventing runaway costs matters more than fine-tuning individual pods. Cron-based scaling operates at the infrastructure level, deprovisioning entire node pools during predictable idle periods. It achieves 65-75% savings on eligible workloads—development, staging, and test environments—because it eliminates the cost

## Summary: From Waste to Efficiency

Every oversized pod in your cluster represents money leaving your infrastructure untouched. Kubernetes defaults to this waste by design—it prioritizes reliability over efficiency, and that safety margin has a price. The path from waste to efficiency follows a proven sequence: measure actual consumption using p95 metrics rather than averages, apply the Vertical Pod Autoscaler to right-size individual pod requests, and establish ResourceQuota as guardrails against aggregate overcommitment. This foundation delivers 20-40% savings on production stateless applications while preventing runaway costs in multi-tenant environments.

For development and staging environments, cron-based scaling provides immediate relief by eliminating entirely idle infrastructure. An e-commerce platform reduced dev/test spending from $89,000 to $52,000 monthly in six weeks; a fintech company achieved $34,000 to $21,000 monthly in four weeks.

With this foundation in place, teams unlock advanced optimizations: Spot instances, Karpenter consolidation, and ARM migration become viable when baseline efficiency is established. The compounding benefit extends beyond cost—efficient resources enable faster scaling and better performance, not just savings.
